author: @bigpaul65
text: Great video as usual however there is minor error at the start when you switched from talking about 1 revolution per second to metres per second, which are not the same.  Turning any size wheel at 1 metre per second will see them travel exactly the same distance with the revolutions per second being different!  (at 1:45 into video)

author: @MrTubeuser12
text: I remember learning this stuff in intermediate school. I guess you could also explain the speed/torque relationship between the input gear and the output gear with respect to the size relationship of those gears, that is, if the output gear is larger than the input gear then the output speed would DECREASE relative to the input gear, but the torque will INCREASE relative to the input gear, another words if the output gear was twice the size of the input gear, then the output would be HALF the speed but have TWICE the torque, and the reverse is true also.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: absolutely mate - but t his already too k20 mins and there is a limit to what can be included - but I agree with what you are saying and it is an important aspect

author: @werxeh8027
text:
This is entirely unrelated to this video, but something I've thought about given the content of your channel and how much you've done on repurposing things recently.

Have you ever considered making something kind of akin to The Hitchhiker's Guide To The Galaxy? I have been thinking of something akin to a voltaic pile battery (with written instructions on how to power it) and an e-ink display to further provide information on power generation, and various other useful information. Then with enough power you could eventually run a catalogue of videos on a low power MP4 playing screen with most of your useful information. Then house it in a solid waterproof box. Not that I expect civilisation to end any time soon, but something like that which could last just seems like a nice thing. Of course SD cards don't hold data indefinitely, so unless there's a better long term storage method it might only be viable for 80-100 years.

replies.author: @werxeh8027
replies.text: With that said, an actual book would probably be better :) You definitely should write a book with basic useful information

author: @sofa-lofa4241
text:
Great video Rob, it was a nice simple breakdown of something that seems hard but doesn't have to be,
I often find nylon gears in all sorts of scrap, from printers to kids toys, a bit of string and a ruler and a calculator will give you the ratio, 

Metal gears for bigger projects can be found in bicycle derailleur systems, with a free chain thrown in! The manufacturers often publish the ratios for each system on their website, or again, a tape measure and a calculator will soon tell you,

The best stuff I have found from scrap has usually been from high quality tools or industrial equipment where the gears are sintered metal or machined from stock and hardened, these can take a bit of 'persuasive force' before giving up, great for high torque applications and when you need things to be 'skookum'

author: @benthere8051
text: Continuously variable transmissions (CVT) have always intrigued me.

replies.author: @ollieb9875
replies.text: Had to buy a new tractor mower recently which has CVT, I say it works well enough and apparently is reliable, the fact one needs to keep the foot pressed is a bit of a pain after a while, the other one we have just has 3 forward here and reverse with a proper clutch 🙃 never driven a car with CVT though. Apparently it's become reliable. One thing about the tractor other than not having a brake apart from the handbrake is basically 2 pedals on the right side, big for forward, small for reverse. Common of CVT I don't know but kind of nice feature.

replies.author: @sofa-lofa4241
replies.text: Yeah, me too, I get the whole 'belt moving on 2 cones' thing, but it's the steel belt itself that does my brain in... engineering black magic right there

replies.author: @GofuKyersen
replies.text: Was it the Trabant had that cone and belt thing in the gearbox? "

replies.author: @MrTubeuser12
replies.text: If you imagine the cones in a CVT, in cross section, basically what you'll is two circles, as the belt moves up one cone and down the other, what you'll see is one circle increasing and the other decreasing(in cross section) so the basic gear ratio relationship between the two "circles" works the same as explained in this video, it's the mechanical implementation that's different and at first glance can be a bit confusing.

replies.author: @totherarf
replies.text: @GofuKyersen I think some of the smaller earlier Volvo's did as well!

author: @benthere8051
text: So the gear ratio is actually the ratio of circumferences.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: yes - which breaks down to the ratio of the radii

replies.author: @vylbird8014
replies.text: On an ideal gear, where the point of contact is at a fixed radius. Perfectly machined and matched gears using an involute tooth profile can achieve that. But less-than-ideal gears will not always make contact at precisely the same ratio, which translated to a rapidly varying gear ratio and lots of noisy vibration and wear.
